name: Aristotle
credit: Roman copy after the bronze original by Lysippos, c. 330 BCE — Palazzo Altemps, Rome
license_guess: PD-old / PD-Art (sculptor Lysippos d. c. 305 BCE; photo of public-domain artwork)
notes: Most-reproduced portrait of Aristotle. Vienna Kunsthistorisches copy is also widely used; either works. Lead infobox image on en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aristotle as of recent revisions.

name: Plato
credit: Roman copy of a portrait by Silanion, c. 370 BCE — Capitoline Museums, Rome
notes: Standard Wikipedia infobox image. Silanion was contemporary with Plato; the surviving copy is Roman.

name: Confucius
credit: Tang Dynasty portrait of Confucius — Wu Daozi (c. 685–758)
notes: Wu Daozi rubbing is the most-reproduced premodern image. An alternative is Kong Zi Sheng Ji Tu (Pictures of the Sage's Traces). Verify exact filename — Commons has multiple versions.

name: René Descartes
credit: Frans Hals, Portrait of René Descartes, c. 1648 — Louvre Museum
notes: The canonical Descartes portrait. Several Hals workshop variants exist; the Louvre version is the standard reference.

name: Thomas Hobbes
credit: John Michael Wright, Portrait of Thomas Hobbes, c. 1669–70 — National Portrait Gallery, London
notes: NPG London holds the canonical late portrait. The Frontispiece engraving from Leviathan (1651) is also widely used and unambiguously PD.

name: John Locke
credit: Godfrey Kneller, Portrait of John Locke, 1697 — Hermitage Museum, St Petersburg
notes: Standard Wikipedia infobox image. Kneller painted Locke twice; the Hermitage version is the most reproduced.

name: David Hume
credit: Allan Ramsay, Portrait of David Hume, 1766 — Scottish National Portrait Gallery, Edinburgh
notes: The definitive Hume portrait by his friend Ramsay. Filename on Commons may vary — check 'Allan Ramsay - David Hume'.

name: Immanuel Kant
credit: Johann Gottlieb Becker, Portrait of Immanuel Kant, 1768
notes: Several attributed portraits exist; the 1768 Becker is the most-reproduced. An alternative is the c. 1790 anonymous portrait at the Schiller-Nationalmuseum.

name: John Stuart Mill
credit: London Stereoscopic Company, photograph of John Stuart Mill, c. 1870 — National Portrait Gallery, London
license_guess: PD (photo over 100 years old, photographer's term expired)
notes: Mill is photographable — use a real photo, not a painting. The London Stereoscopic Co. albumen print is the canonical image.

name: Friedrich Nietzsche
credit: Friedrich Hermann Hartmann, photograph of Friedrich Nietzsche, 1875 (or Gustav-Adolf Schultze, 1882)
notes: Many Nietzsche photos exist on Commons. Avoid the Hans Olde 1899 photographs of him in mental decline — use the prime-of-life images. The 1882 Schultze portrait is widely used.

name: Søren Kierkegaard
credit: Niels Christian Kierkegaard, sketch of Søren Kierkegaard, c. 1840 (cousin of the philosopher)
notes: Few authentic likenesses exist — Kierkegaard refused most portraits. The cousin's sketch is the standard. Some sources attribute a similar portrait to Christian Albrecht Jensen.

name: Bertrand Russell
credit: Bassano Ltd, photograph of Bertrand Russell, 1916 — National Portrait Gallery, London
license_guess: PD (UK photo > 70 years post-publication; pre-1929 US public domain)
notes: Russell lived to 1970, so newer photos are still copyrighted. Use a 1910s–1930s photo. Bassano studio portraits are well-documented PD.

name: Jean-Paul Sartre
credit: Photograph of Jean-Paul Sartre, 1967 — by Dutch National Archives (Anefo)
notes: Anefo photos of Sartre and Beauvoir during their 1967 visit to the Netherlands are CC-licensed. Avoid Cartier-Bresson images (© Magnum).

name: Simone de Beauvoir
credit: Photograph of Simone de Beauvoir, 1967 — by Dutch National Archives (Anefo) / Moshe Milner
notes: Same Anefo 1967 series as Sartre. There's also an Israeli Government Press Office image from her 1967 visit that's CC-BY-SA.

name: Philippa Foot
credit: Photograph of Philippa Foot — source to be selected by teacher
license_guess: verify — likely CC-BY-SA if from Wikipedia
notes: Foot is harder; her Wikipedia article uses a non-free image under fair use. Possible alternatives: a Somerville College Oxford archive photo (request use), or contact the Foot estate. Some lecture photos are CC. May need teacher's institutional contact for a clean source.

name: G. E. M. Anscombe
credit: Photograph of G. E. M. Anscombe — source to be selected by teacher
license_guess: verify — Wikipedia likely uses fair use
notes: Like Foot, Anscombe's Wikipedia image is fair-use. For a CC-licensed alternative, check the Wittgenstein Archive Cambridge or the Anscombe Bioethics Centre. The teacher's institutional fair-use coverage probably permits the Wikipedia image.

name: Iris Murdoch
credit: Photograph of Iris Murdoch — source to be selected by teacher
notes: Murdoch's Wikipedia image is also typically fair-use. Ida Kar's 1957 photograph at NPG London is the canonical likeness; check NPG's CC-BY-NC offerings or whether the Kar portrait is now PD (Kar d. 1974).

name: Bernard Williams
credit: Photograph of Bernard Williams — source to be selected by teacher
notes: Steve Pyke's portrait is iconic but © Pyke. Wikipedia uses fair use. King's College Cambridge or All Souls Oxford may have institutional photos available for educational use.

name: Derek Parfit
credit: Photograph of Derek Parfit — source to be selected by teacher
notes: Steve Pyke's Parfit portrait is iconic but copyrighted. Wikipedia's image is fair-use. All Souls College Oxford or Rutgers (where he taught) may have permissioned photos.

name: Frank Jackson
credit: Photograph of Frank Jackson — Australian National University staff photo or similar
notes: Living philosopher; ANU and Princeton (where he visits) may have permissioned headshots. As an educator at an Australian institution, the teacher may have direct access to ANU's image library.

name: Martha Nussbaum
credit: Photograph of Martha Nussbaum — Wikipedia user submission, October 2010
notes: Nussbaum has multiple CC-BY-SA images on Commons from her many lectures. The 2010 one is widely used; newer talks have newer photos.

name: Carol Gilligan
credit: Photograph of Carol Gilligan — NYU faculty profile or conference snapshot
notes: Gilligan is harder to source CC images for. NYU Steinhardt may have a permissioned faculty photo. Some conference recordings have stills that authors release CC-BY.

name: Peter Singer
credit: Photograph of Peter Singer at MIT Veritas Forum, 2009
notes: Singer has the most CC-licensed photos of any living philosopher on this list — he speaks publicly often. Multiple high-quality options on Commons; pick a flattering recent one.

name: Slavoj Žižek
credit: Mariusz Kubik, photograph of Slavoj Žižek in Liverpool, 2008
license_guess: CC-BY-SA-3.0 (photographer Mariusz Kubik widely releases CC-BY)
notes: Mariusz Kubik's Žižek portraits are the canonical Wikipedia images and are explicitly CC-BY-SA. Multiple poses available.
